coredata.dc:title: Persulfate mediated single-channel bimodal self-checking light-assisted self-powered aptasensor for highly accurate assay
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xingyu
Du, Xiaojiao
Sun, Jun
Zhang, Bing
Lian, Yuebin
Zheng, Min
Zhu, Rongquan
Geng, Long
coredata.dc:description: Developing multiple signals are needed for self-powered sensors to improve the detection accuracy. Herein we designed a novel dual-power-peak photofuel cell (PFC) based self-powered sensors with persulfate as an initiator. Persulfate expands photocatalytic redox reactions of PFC system, which enhanced separation of electrons and holes and more importantly, reduced the ohmic loss (η ohmic) efficiency and formed a second power peak for the first time. Such a self-powered sensor could self-check by using two separate power peaks in the same signal-readout window, greatly improving the accuracy compared with a single-peak-signal one. Moreover, the introduction of V2C MXene further accelerated the electron transfer and improved the photoelectric conversion efficiency. The aptamer was immobilized on the photoanode for the specific detection of the model analyte chloramphenicol and the reliable quantification was achieved via dual-power-peak density values from the single photoanode interface. Under optimum conditions, the linear range of this self-powered aptasensor was 0.5 pM to 3 nM, with a detection limit of 0.17 pM. As a result, a novel self-powered aptasensor was developed successfully based on the strategy of persulfate induced dual-power-peak, showing great promise in the development of multi-signal sensors with high accuracy and specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-powered sensors
Dual-photoelectrode
K2S2O8
Dual-power-peak
Self-check

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the BiVO4/fluoride doped tin oxide (FTO) photoanode
The self-powered sensing process of CAP
Characterization of BiVO4 and MoOHCF nanostructures
Characterization of the PSPS fabrication and the operating mechanism of self-powered sensing
Exploring the crucial effect of K2S2O8
Analytical performance of the dual-power-peak PSPS for CAP detection
Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the fabricated PSPS
Detection of CAP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31971788 , 22202020 and 21904015 ), the Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Fund (Nos. CX(23)3049 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20211062 ). The authors also would like to express thanks to the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project ( 2020M671366 and 2020M681525 ), Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX23_3660 ), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China Funded Major Project ( 21KJA610001 ), Changzhou Science and Technology Support Project ( CE20230583 and CE20215047 ), and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project of Jiangsu Province ( 2020Z266 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of BiVO4. Inset: the SEM image of BiVO4 at high magnification. (B) SEM image of MoOHCF. Inset: the SEM image of MoOHCF at high magnification. (C) XRD pattern of (a) BiVO4 and (b) MoOHCF. (D) Nyquist plots of (a) FTO, (b) BiVO4/FTO, (c) V2C/BiVO4/FTO, (d) aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO, and (e) aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO incubated with 3 μM CAP. (E) Polarization curves of (a) aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO and (b) MoOHCF/FTO under photoirradiation in PBS at 10 mV/s. (F) P-I curve of the PSPS modified process: (a) BiVO4/FTO in the dark, (b) BiVO4/FTO under the photoirradiation without K2S2O8, (c) BiVO4/FTO, (d) V2C/BiVO4/FTO, (e) aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO and (f) aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO incubated with 0.01 nM CAP under photoirradiation with 11 mM K2S2O8.
Effect of SO4•− scavenger (ETOH) on (A) V-I curve, Eocv and (B) P-I curve; Effect of H+ on V-I curve and P-I curve: (C) adding NaOH and (D) adding HCl.

(A) P–I curves of the dual-power-peak PSPS incubated with CAP at different concentrations (from a to f): 0.0005, 0.002, 0.02, 0.1, 0.5, and 3 μM. (B) calibration curves for CAP detection by the dual-power-peak PSPS with 11 mM K2S2O8.

(A) P Max1 and (B) P Max2 values of the dual-power-peak PSPS to 10−9 M of CAP and 10−6 M of other interfering substances: (a) CAP, (b) Mixture, (c) Blank, (d) KAN, (e) ENR, (f) TET, (g) AMP, (h) TOB, (i) NEO, and (j) STR. Experimental conditions: PBS containing 11 mM K2S2O8.

(A) Fabrication of the photoanode. (B) Preparation of the photocathode. (C) Construction of the dual-power-peak PSPS. (D) Schematic illustration of the dual-power-peak detection configuration for the highly sensitive and accurate self-powered assay.

head.ce:title: Persulfate mediated single-channel bimodal self-checking light-assisted self-powered aptasensor for highly accurate assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Photoelectric Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Photoelectric Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Photoelectric Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, Changzhou, Jiangsu, 213032, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: They contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing multiple signals are needed for self-powered sensors to improve the detection accuracy. Herein we designed a novel dual-power-peak photofuel cell (PFC) based self-powered sensors with persulfate as an initiator. Persulfate expands photocatalytic redox reactions of PFC system, which enhanced separation of electrons and holes and more importantly, reduced the ohmic loss (η ohmic) efficiency and formed a second power peak for the first time. Such a self-powered sensor could self-check by using two separate power peaks in the same signal-readout window, greatly improving the accuracy compared with a single-peak-signal one. Moreover, the introduction of V2C MXene further accelerated the electron transfer and improved the photoelectric conversion efficiency. The aptamer was immobilized on the photoanode for the specific detection of the model analyte chloramphenicol and the reliable quantification was achieved via dual-power-peak density values from the single photoanode interface. Under optimum conditions, the linear range of this self-powered aptasensor was 0.5 pM to 3 nM, with a detection limit of 0.17 pM. As a result, a novel self-powered aptasensor was developed successfully based on the strategy of persulfate induced dual-power-peak, showing great promise in the development of multi-signal sensors with high accuracy and specificity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A single-channel dual-power-peak self-checking self-powered sensor was developed with high sensitivity and accuracy, which can realize two separate power peak as detection signals in the same window for the first time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Self-powered sensor has attracted enormous research attention due to no need of external power supply lately, which is easy for the miniaturization and cost-effectiveness of sensors [1–5]. Nowadays, it possesses different types, including fuel cell (FC)-based self-powered electrochemical sensor (SPES) and nanogenerator-based sensors [6]. Specifically, the typical FC can be prepared using a photoanode for oxidation of fuel and a cathode for reduction of electron acceptor. The simultaneous reaction of anodic oxidation and cathodic reduction can produce electrical energy, while the open circuit potential (OCP) or output power density (OPD) of FCs is related to the interaction between targets and electrodes of sensors [7,8]. Among them, since Zhang’s group developed the first photocatalytic fuel cell based self-powered sensor (PSPS) using Ni(OH)2/CdS/TiO2 photoanode and hemin graphene cathode in 2016 [9], PSPS which could convert the multi-dimensional energy (the light and chemical energy) into electricity simultaneously, and thus create more energy utilization, has been extensively applied in self-powered sensing of ultratrace analytes. For example, Wang’s group developed a dual-photoelectrode PSPS which was composed of a B–TiO2 photoanode and a CuBr photocathode in a single-chamber for digoxin monitoring [10]. Jiang’s group successfully fabricated a PSPS for the sensitive detection of microcystin-LR, which demonstrated its promise in on-site test and portable device owing to no need of external power source [11].

Self-checking has been regarded as an efficient way to improve the detection reliability in sensing areas [12]. The first dual-signal PSPS combining the photocurrent response and power density-current response (P-I) was reported in 2019 [13]. It quantifies target species with two signals instead of a single-signal and thus can intrinsically correct the influence of systematic error [14]. When the dual-signal output sensing strategy is in collaboration with the output electrical signal of self-powered sensors, an accurate and reliable PSPS with two sensing signals can be achieved. So far, two strategies including dual-mode and spatial-resolution have been proposed to realize dual-signal output sensing of PSPS [15,16]. For instance, Yan’s group developed a dual-mode PSPS by using photocurrent and OPD as two different types of electrical signals for the accurate detection of microcystin-LR [17]. Zhang’s group designed a spatial-resolution technique induced dual-channel PSPS with two separate OPD plots as analysis signals for the 17β-Estradiol determination [3]. Some advances have been made in the construction of highly accurate PSPS, but the output signals are in the different signal-readout windows/channels and encountered with the problems: (1) complex instrument or operating process such as two modes or channels involved alternating measurement or specially designed sensing interface; (2) the deviation or errors from different detection signal-readout windows due to an asynchronous detection process.

As is known to all, for electrochemical sensors, two separated redox peak signals in the same signal-readout window have been well studied to achieve the simultaneous signal-readout detection by introducing two independent probes [18]. For fluorescence sensors, two or more emission intensity changes at different wavelengths can be applied for the same window typed simultaneous detection [19]. Inspired by these, if two detection signals can be collected from the same signal-readout window for PSPS, it would be definitely more convenient and concise, and can overcome above-mentioned obstacles. However, similar studies in PSPS are few because it seems very difficult to provide more signal forms for the accurate assay especially multiple signals in a single signal-readout window. Therefore, it is meaningful to explore the design of PSPS with two separated peak signals in the same signal window, because it would obviously improve the detection accuracy.

In this work, a novel dual-power-peak typed PSPS coupling with potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) was proposed for assay in high accuracy ( Scheme 1). K2S2O8, a kind of reactive and persistent oxidants, made full use of the surface space of photoelectrodes in the PFC system to expand photocatalytic redox reactions, vastly reduced the ohmic loss (η ohmic) efficiency of PFC at middle current region and thus successfully formed a second power peak. Using chloramphenicol (CAP) as a concept-to-proof study, a dual-power-peak PSPS was built to study the analytical performances. When the CAP-binding aptamer was immobilized on the photoanode, the output signal of PSPS including two power-peaks changed sensitively with different concentrations of CAP. On the other hand, considering that a typical PSPS comprising of a n-type semiconductor photoanode and a noble metal cathode suffered from high cost, this PSPS used molybdate hexacyanoferrate (MoOHCF) as photocathode instead of a precious metal. Moreover, the adherence of vanadium carbide (V2C) MXene further accelerated electricity generation and improved the sensing performance of the PSPS. The present work not only provides a sensitive, portable, and low-cost method for target monitoring but also provides a new strategy for establishing a dual-signal PSPS with high accuracy.
Materials and reagents can be seen in detail in the Supporting information.
Apparatus used in this work are provided in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the BiVO4/fluoride doped tin oxide (FTO) photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the BiVO4/FTO photoanode can be seen in detail in the Supporting information.
The preparation process of the MoOHCF/FTO photocathode can be seen in detail in the Supporting information.

The construction of dual-power-peak PSPS was implemented by integrating a modified V2C/BiVO4/FTO photoanode and a MoOHCF/FTO photocathode in a single-compartment cell illuminated by the light source. For photoanode, 10 μL of 0.1 % chitosan (CHIT) was drip-coated on the surface of BiVO4/FTO. Then, 10 μL of V2C MXene was modified on the electrode, and the electrical conductivity of photoanode was increased with the improvement of electrons scattering. To reinforce the stability, the film was baked at for 30 min under an infrared lamp and completely washed with ultrapure water. Afterwards, 10 μL of 5 μM CAP-binding aptamer was dropped on the obtained electrode and incubated at 60 ℃ for 6 h. Finally, the free aptamer molecules were cleaned with ultrapure water, and the aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO photoanode was prepared. For electrolyte, 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.0) containing K2S2O8 was added into a quartz single-chamber reaction cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The self-powered sensing process of CAP
ce:section.ce:para: The modified photoanode was incubated with 0.1 M PBS containing different concentrations of CAP at room temperature for 1 h. Afterwards, the resulting photoanode was washed with ultrapure water and measured in the single-compartment cell to collect the detection signal of certain concentrations of CAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BiVO4 and MoOHCF nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared BiVO4 nanostructures was observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As can be seen in Fig. 1A, BiVO4 crystals was uniformly distributed on the FTO substrate. Further, BiVO4 crystals with a diameter of 100–200 nm overlapped each other to form a coral bar-like architecture. The crystalline structure of the BiVO4/FTO photoanode was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements. As shown in Fig. 1C, the diffraction peaks of BiVO4 were located at 2θ of 18.6°, 28.9°, 37.8°, and 45.6°, corresponding to the (110), (121), (220), and (231) planes of monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS card No. 14–0688), respectively [20].

The morphology of MoOHCF was characterized by SEM. Fig. 1B suggested that the MoOHCF film showed a cluster-like architecture. From XRD patterns (curve b in Fig. 1C), except the diffraction peaks of SnO2 from FTO glass, some diffused peaks were attributed to MoOHCF (JCPDS No. 41–1476). Although these peaks were not very clear, they had the similar position compared to the confirmed peaks of FeHCF, illustrating that MoOHCF is a kind of hexacyanoferrate [21].
The band-gap structure of BiVO4 and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of two electrodes can be seen in detail in the supporting information.

To verify the modification of the photoanode successfully, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the redox probe. The diameter of the Nyquist plot showed charge transfer resistance (Ret) of different modified electrodes. Compared with bare FTO (curve a in Fig. 1D), the BiVO4/FTO electrode exhibited a smaller Ret which was estimated to be 156 Ω (curve b in Fig. 1D). After the decoration of the BiVO4/FTO electrode with V2C nanoflakes, the Ret value decreased to 142 Ω (curve c in Fig. 1D), which was attributed to the excellent conductivity of V2C nanoflakes [22]. When the CAP aptamer was immobilized on V2C/BiVO4/FTO, the Ret value distinctly increased to 225 Ω (curve d in Fig. 1D), ascribing to the higher resistance of the aptamer which hindered the negatively charged redox probe of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− approaching the electrode surface and impeded the transfer of electrons. Once the CAP molecules was incubated with the aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO photoanode, an obviously reduced value of Ret was obtained (curve e in Fig. 1D) due to the the CAP-induced conformational change of the aptamer. The conformational change could reduce the binding between the aptamer and the electrode surface, which allowed a part of aptamer-CAP complexes to dissociate from the electrode surface [23]. These results of EIS indicated that the photoanode was modified successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the PSPS fabrication and the operating mechanism of self-powered sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The polarization curves for the modified photoanode and photocathode were used to verify the thermodynamic feasibility of the constructed PSPS. As can be seen, the onset oxidation potential of H2O on aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO appeared at ∼ −0.39 V under light illumination (curve a in Fig. 1E); meanwhile, the electrocatalytic reduction of oxygen on the MoOHCF/FTO started at 0.38 V (curve b in Fig. 1E). The oxidation of water was catalyzed by the aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO photoanode at a relatively low potential while the reduction of oxygen was catalyzed by the MoOHCF/FTO photocathode at a high potential, indicating the thermodynamic feasibility of the proposed PSPS [24].

Moreover, the P-I curves of different modified processes were recorded to assess the output performance of the constructed PSPS. As can be seen in Fig. 1F, the PSPS which was constructed with BiVO4/FTO as photoanode, MoOHCF/FTO as cathode and 0.1 M PBS as electrolyte showed a maximum output power density (PMax) of 0.005 μW cm−2 in the dark (curve a in Fig. 1F); whereas, the PMax became 0.463 μW cm−2 under light irridiation (curve b in Fig. 1F), indicating that the hydroxide ions (OH-) or water molecules (H2O) was difficult to oxidize on the photoanode in the dark. A higher PMax was obtained when the photoanode was under light irridiation, which was attributed to the photo-generated holes promoting the oxidation of OH- and H2O [17]. After K2S2O8 was introduced in the electrolyte, the PMax value increased to 0.882 μW cm−2 (curve c in Fig. 1F) and surprisingly, special dual-power peaks were observed for the first time in the PSPS. Afterward, the PMax value further increased after modified with V2C (curve d in Fig. 1F), demonstrating that V2C successfully enhanced electrical conductivity of the PSPS. However, with immobilizing aptamer on the photoanode, the PMax value decreased to 0.626 μW cm−2 (curve e in Fig. 1F) due to the increased steric hindrance. Remarkably, when 0.01 nM CAP was incubated with the photoanode aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO, the PMax value increased to 0.774 μW cm−2 (curve f in Fig. 1F). Because the CAP molecules could be captured by aptamer and oxidized by holes, superoxide radicals (O2•-), sulfate radicals (SO4•-) and active hydroxide radicals (•OH), thus inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [25].
The operating mechanism of self-powered sensing were investigated in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the crucial effect of K2S2O8
ce:section.ce:para: Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra (Fig. S3) and SO4•- quenching test ( Fig. 2A-B) strongly demonstrated that the high OCP induced by K2S2O8 activation is the primary reason for the generation of the first power peak. Meanwhile, H+ enhancement and H+ quenching tests (Fig. 2C-D) proved that the H+ formed in the S2O8 2- conversion will weaken the η ohmic efficiency of PFC, opening up a new way for the generation of a second power peak at middle current region [26]. The details of the experimental process were stated in the Supporting Information.
To obtain a more ideal signal response, the main experimental conditions were optimized during the construction process and seen in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the dual-power-peak PSPS for CAP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ability of the proposed PSPS for detecting CAP with dual-power peaks, the output power of the PSPS was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3A, the intensity of two power peaks increased with the increasing CAP concentration. This could be explained that many aptamer-CAP complexes were produced on the photoanode and oxidized by the photogenerated holes, •O2 -, SO4•- and •OH, leading to the increment of the V oc, I sc and power density (Fig. S12A-B) [27,28]. As indicated in Fig. 3B, the P Max1 and P Max2 were linearly correlated with the logarithm of CAP concentration from 0.5 pM to 3 nM. The linear regression equations were P Max1 = 0.0994 logCCAP+ 1.044 and P Max2 = 0.0639 logCCAP + 0.6541, the correlation coefficients R2 were 0.9737 and 0.9993, and the detection limit of both linear equations was 0.17 pmol/L (S/N = 3). The accuracy of the detection result was evaluated by determining whether the corresponding relationship of "PMax1 - analyte concentration - PMax2" was established [29]. For example, when testing the real sample, if the PSPS exhibits that PMax1 is 0.875 μW, then according to the linear regression equation of PMax1, the CAP concentration can be obtained as 0.02 μM. Afterwards, through the linear regression equation of PMax2, the predicted PMax2 corresponding to 0.02 μM CAP can be acquired as 0.533 μW. If the actual readout of PMax2 match the predicted data, the test result is credible; otherwise, the sample needs to be retested. In order to highlight advantages of the dual-power-peak PSPS, a single-signal PSPS was tested and the results can be seen in detail in the Supporting information.

Compared with the reported detection methods for CAP (Table S2), the proposed strategy exhibited a relatively wide linear response range, a lower detection limit, and did not require a complex and expensive molecular labelling procedures. In addition, the dual power peak strategy of the proposed PSPS make the self-check function possible, improving the accuracy of the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the fabricated PSPS
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the dual-power-peak PSPS was investigated by measuring the power responses of seven typical interfering antibiotics, such as kanamycin (KAN), enrofloxacin (ENR), tetracycline (TET), ampicillin (AMP), tobramycin (TOB), neomycin (NEO) and streptomycin (STR). According to Fig. 4, unlike target CAP, other typical interfering antibiotics did not show an apparent power intensity change in the PMax1 and PMax2 when compared with the blank solution. The experimental result attested that the dual-power-peak PSPS had satisfactory specificity for the detection of CAP.

The stability is also significant for PSPS. Fig. S14A recorded the power responses of the dual-power-peak PSPS in CAP detection every 2 days. The dual-power signal still maintained 94.01 % and 97.99 % of the original signals after 6 cycles, demonstrating the acceptable stability.

To study the reproducibility of the dual-power-peak PSPS, five independently synthesized aptamer/V2C/BiVO4/FTO electrodes were prepared for the measurement of power responses, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of dual-power peaks were achieved as 0.87 % and 1.4 % (Fig. S14B), which certified that the dual-power-peak PSPS exhibited the admirable repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CAP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To study the detection ability of the dual-power-peak PSPS in real samples, the standard addition method was employed to determine CAP in pond water and milk samples. The pond water sample collected from Changzhou City was centrifuged at 2000 rpM for 10 min in the centrifuge to remove the solid sediment. At last, the supernatant was taken out and the CAP concentration was measured using the dual-power-peak PSPS. As shown in Table S3, the CAP content was detected in the sample without adding any concentrations of CAP, indicating that the selected pond water has been contaminated by CAP. It can be seen from the table that the recovery values of CAP were between 98.8 % and 100.4 %, which indicated that the proposed dual-power-peak PSPS had great feasibility to determine CAP in pond water.

The milk sample purchased from a local supermarket was pretreated according to a previous report [30]. First, a certain amount of acetic acid (20 wt %) was added into the milk sample with stirring until the pH down to 4.6. Next, the sample was incubated at 45 °C for 10 min to precipitate casein. Then, the obtained sample was centrifuged twice for 10 min at 10,000 rpm to remove coagulation of proteins and fats. Afterward, different concentrations of CAP were dispersed into the extraction to prepare the mixture. Finally, the mixture was filtered with a organic microporous membrane, followed by adjusting to neutral pH. As demonstrated in Table S3, the recovery values of CAP ranged from 98.4 % to 103.6 %, which indicated that the proposed dual-power-peak PSPS could detect CAP in a milk sample with the acceptable sensitivity and accuracy.

In summary, a single-channel bimodal self-checking PSPS for accurate CAP detection was successfully developed by utilizing K2S2O8 as the initiator and self-powered sensor as the signal source. When K2S2O8 was coupled with the self-powered sensor, the output power was triggered to form two different power peaks on the high potential-low current and low potential-high current states respectively in a single channel. Because the high conductivity of V2C, an effective PMax enhancement were attained. By utilizing the increased effect on the intensity of dual-power-peak by CAP, which was captured by the CAP-binding aptamer, a dual-power-peak PSPS could be structured successfully. The proposed dual-power-peak PSPS exhibited good selectivity, stability and reproducibility. This work not only avoids time-consuming tests and expensive instrumentation but also significantly improves the accuracy by the advantages of dual-signal self-checking strategy. It offers a new method for establishing a dual-signal FC based self-powered sensors with high accuracy and speciﬁcity.

Xingyu Ji: Writing, Validation, Resources, and Writing-Original Draft Preparation. Xiaojiao Du: Conceptualization, Methodology, Review & Editing, Resources, Funding Acquisition, and Project administration. Jun Sun: Funding Acquisition and Supervision. Bing Zhang: Resources and Supervision. Yuebin Lian: Theoretical and Technical supports. Min Zheng: Project administration. Rongquan Zhu: Investigation. Long Geng: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31971788, 22202020 and 21904015), the Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Fund (Nos. CX(23)3049) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211062). The authors also would like to express thanks to the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2020M671366 and 2020M681525), Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_3660), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China Funded Major Project (21KJA610001), Changzhou Science and Technology Support Project (CE20230583 and CE20215047), and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project of Jiangsu Province (2020Z266).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134647.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-powered sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer-coupled graphitic carbon nitride photoanode for selective detection of bisphenol A
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yan, Y.H. Yang, J.D. Zhang, A self-powered sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer-coupled graphitic carbon nitride photoanode for selective detection of bisphenol A, Sens. Actuators, B, 259 (2018) 394–401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-powered photoelectrochemical aptasensor based on dual-photoelectrode photofuel cell for chloramphenicol detection
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.L. Zhang, B. Peng, X.L. Ouyang, X. Zhu, L. Chen, X.Y. Fan, Z.P. Zhou, J.J. Wang, L. Tang, A self-powered photoelectrochemical aptasensor based on dual-photoelectrode photofuel cell for chloramphenicol detection, Sens. Actuators, B, 368 (2022) 132144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric self-powered sensor for 17β-estradiol detection based on a dual-channel photocatalytic fuel cell
Anal. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xingyu Ji is currently a graduate student at Jiangsu University. His main research interest lies in the field of electrochemical sensors.

Xiaojiao Du obtained her Ph.D. in Agricultural Engineering from Jiangsu University (China) in 2018. She was a Visiting Scholar at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University. She is currently an Associate Professor in Changzhou Institute of Technology. Her current research interests include the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in pesticides and toxin analysis in agricultural products.

Jun Sun is currently a Professor at School of Electrical and Information Engineering, Jiangsu University. He received his Ph.D. in Mechanical and Electronic Engineering from Jiangsu University (China) in 2005. He is mainly focused on the designing nondestructive testing technology and equipment for agricultural products.
Bing Zhang is currently a Professor in Changzhou Institute of Technology. His main research interest is design of the equipment for agricultural products
Yuebin Lian is currently a lecturer in Changzhou Institute of Technology. His main research interests are electrocatalytic and metal-air battery.
Min Zheng is currently a lecturer in Changzhou Institute of Technology. Her main research interest is the design and preparation of the nanomaterials with high photoelectric conversion efficiency.
Rongquan Zhu is currently a graduate student at Jiangsu University. His main research interest lies in the field of electrochemical sensors.
Long Geng is currently a graduate student at Jiangsu University. His current research is on electrochemical self-powered sensors.
